Use camelCase for local variables and private fields (e.g., `int itemCount`, `private string _userName`).
Use PascalCase for public properties, constants, and static readonly fields (e.g., `public int MaxRetries`).
Prefix private backing fields with an underscore (e.g., `private readonly ILogger _logger`).
Use `var` when the type is obvious from the right-hand side of the assignment (e.g., `var list = new List<string>()`).
Use explicit types when the type is not immediately clear from context to improve readability.
Declare variables with the narrowest possible scope.
Prefer `const` for compile-time constants and `static readonly` for runtime constants.
Use meaningful, descriptive names that convey intent (e.g., `customerCount` instead of `n`).
Use nullable reference types (`string?`) and enable the `<Nullable>enable</Nullable>` project setting.
Prefer `readonly` on fields that should not be reassigned after construction.
Use `record` types or `init`-only setters for immutable data models where appropriate.
